title: Can MACE Potentials Accurately Describe Magnetism and Phase Stability in Fe-Ni Alloys? A Systematic Benchmark
authors: Kushal Ramakrishna, Mani Lokamani, Attila Cangi
abstract: We present a systematic benchmark of MACE potentials for iron-nickel alloys, focusing on structural, elastic, magnetic, and finite-temperature properties relevant to phase stability. The reference dataset comprises spin-polarized PBE density functional theory (DFT) calculations for chemically disordered special quasirandom structures (SQS), spanning compositions, bcc and fcc crystal structures, and volumetric and shear deformations. A system-specific MACE-sqs model trained on this dataset achieves validation errors of 2.0 meV/atom for energies and 24.3 meV/Angstrom for forces. Compared with several MACE foundation models, including models trained with Hubbard U corrections, MACE-sqs gives the most consistent agreement with DFT and experiment for equations of state, equilibrium volumes, elastic constants, and thermal expansion trends in bcc and fcc Fe-Ni alloys. For the bcc-to-hcp transition, MACE-sqs predicts a pure-Fe transition pressure closer to experiment than the tested foundation models, but all models predict an incorrect increase of transition pressure with Ni content. This failure indicates that high-pressure magnetic collapse and composition-dependent magnetoelastic effects are not yet fully captured. Overall, targeted SQS-based training substantially improves the accuracy of MACE potentials for Fe-Ni alloys, while phase stability under magnetic collapse remains a key limitation for future model development.
summary: This paper demonstrates that training MACE machine-learning potentials on system-specific disordered structures (SQS) significantly improves the description of magnetism and elasticity in Fe-Ni alloys compared to general foundation models. However, it reveals that current models still struggle to capture complex high-pressure magnetic collapse and composition-dependent phase transitions.
main_problem: Evaluating the accuracy and transferability of MACE machine-learned interatomic potentials in describing magnetism, phase stability, and elastic properties in chemically disordered Fe-Ni alloys.
main_result: A system-specific MACE-sqs model outperforms general foundation models in capturing equilibrium volumes, elastic constants, and thermal expansion, though it fails to correctly predict the composition-dependent bcc-to-hcp transition pressure.
method: The study uses spin-polarized PBE density functional theory (DFT) on Special Quasirandom Structures (SQS) to train a MACE potential, which is then benchmarked against experimental data and various MACE foundation models.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of chemically disordered Fe-Ni alloys in bcc and fcc crystal structures, including Invar-like compositions, modeled using SQS to represent random atomic distributions.
key_observables: Energies, forces, equations of state, equilibrium volumes, elastic constants (C11, C12, C44), magnetic moments, and thermal expansion coefficients.
important_parameters: Ni concentration (%), pressure (GPa), temperature (K), and Warren-Cowley short-range order parameters.
main_assumptions: The training uses PBE-level DFT without Hubbard U corrections; the models assume the ability to capture magnetoelastic effects through local chemical environments without explicit spin-disorder physics.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows SRO parameters vs concentration; Fig 2 shows training data, magnetic moments, and energy/force parity plots; Fig 3 compares EOS curves; Fig 4 shows atomic volume vs Ni content; Fig 5 shows bcc-hcp transition pressure; Fig 6 compares elastic constants; Fig 7 shows temperature-dependent lattice parameters and thermal expansion.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of MLIP accuracy in magnetic alloys, describes the SQS-based training methodology, presents a systematic benchmark of structural, elastic, and thermal properties, discusses the failure in phase stability transitions, and concludes with future directions for capturing magnetic collapse.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Warren–Cowley short–range order (SRO) parameters for Fe–Ni alloys as a function of nickel concentration for bcc (left) and fcc (right) structures. Marker color denotes the system size N, and marker shape denotes the SRO type (Fe–Ni, Ni–Fe, Ni–Ni, Fe–Fe). Larger system sizes yield SRO values closer to zero, indicating a more random chemical distribution, whereas deviations reflect clustering or ordering tendencies.

FIG. 2. Overview of the training dataset and MACE model performance. Top left: Representative SQS supercells of Fe–Ni alloys from the training dataset, shown under three deformation conditions: undeformed reference (top), isotropic volumetric strain (middle), and shear strain (bottom). Blue and orange spheres represent Fe and Ni atoms, respectively. Top right: Mean magnetic moment per atom as a function of nickel concentration for fcc (upper) and bcc (lower) Fe–Ni alloys, computed from collinear spin-polarized DFT with the PBE XC functional and compared against experimental measurements [52–61]. Bottom row: Parity plots of MACE predicted energies (left, RMSE = 2.2/2.0 meV/atom) and forces...

FIG. 3. Equation of state (EOS) for Fe–Ni compositions, comparing MACE models with DFT and experiments [4, 8, 12, 68–71]. Note that the EOS results of Zhou et al. [70] correspond to a slightly sulfur–bearing alloy (Fe0.89Ni0.09S0.02).
FIG. 4. Volume per atom of Fe–Ni alloys for bcc (left) and fcc (right) structures as a function of nickel concentration, comparing MACE models with experimental data [4, 8, 59, 72–76].
FIG. 5. Transition pressure from the bcc to hcp structure as a function of nickel concentration, comparing MACE models with experiments [7, 8, 10].
